summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the birth of his son Tobin Cormac Gaffikin Cantrill on September 9th, 2004. He reflects on how the experience of having a newborn humbles him as someone who has spent his career working with complex engineered systems. The post uses the occasion to take a jab at artificial intelligence research, noting that his newborn accomplished more in his first thirty minutes than AI has in 45 years.
key_points: Tobin was born September 9th, 2004 at 8:59am, weighing 8 pounds 11.5 ounces
The complexity of biological systems humbles engineers who work with man-made systems
Cantrill uses the birth to critique the hubris of strong AI research
He planned to return to work on October 4th with DTrace updates
money_quotes: Having a baby is a humbling experience for anyone, but it's especially so when you've spent most of your life dealing with complex man-made systems: it boggles the mind that a system so incredibly intricate could ever work at all, let alone so reliably.
Tobin accomplished more in his first thirty minutes of life than AI has accomplished in 45 years -- and he's certainly a hell of a lot cuter than your average AI lab rat.
key_insight: The staggering complexity of biological systems like a newborn baby should humble engineers and serve as a reality check against the grandiose claims of artificial intelligence research.
